speaker: CLEON
text: My Dionyza, shall we rest us here

speaker: CLEON
text: And, by relating tales of others’ griefs,

speaker: CLEON
text: See if ’twill teach us to forget our own?

speaker: DIONYZA
text: That were to blow at fire in hope to quench it;

speaker: DIONYZA
text: For who digs hills because they do aspire

speaker: DIONYZA
text: Throws down one mountain to cast up a higher.

speaker: DIONYZA
text: O, my distressèd lord, even such our griefs are.

speaker: DIONYZA
text: Here they are but felt, and seen with mischief’s eyes,

speaker: DIONYZA
text: But like to groves, being topped, they higher rise.

speaker: CLEON
text: O Dionyza,

speaker: CLEON
text: Who wanteth food, and will not say he wants it,

speaker: CLEON
text: Or can conceal his hunger till he famish?

speaker: CLEON
text: Our tongues and sorrows do sound deep our woes

speaker: CLEON
text: Into the air, our eyes do weep till lungs

speaker: CLEON
text: Fetch breath that may proclaim them louder, that

speaker: CLEON
text: If heaven slumber while their creatures want,

speaker: CLEON
text: They may awake their helpers to comfort them.

speaker: CLEON
text: I’ll then discourse our woes, felt several years,

speaker: CLEON
text: And, wanting breath to speak, help me with tears.

speaker: DIONYZA
text: I’ll do my best, sir.

speaker: CLEON
text: This Tarsus, o’er which I have the government,

speaker: CLEON
text: A city on whom Plenty held full hand,

speaker: CLEON
text: For Riches strewed herself even in her streets;

speaker: CLEON
text: Whose towers bore heads so high they kissed the clouds,

speaker: CLEON
text: And strangers ne’er beheld but wondered at;

speaker: CLEON
text: Whose men and dames so jetted and adorned,

speaker: CLEON
text: Like one another’s glass to trim them by;

speaker: CLEON
text: Their tables were stored full to glad the sight,

speaker: CLEON
text: And not so much to feed on as delight;

speaker: CLEON
text: All poverty was scorned, and pride so great,

speaker: CLEON
text: The name of help grew odious to repeat.

speaker: DIONYZA
text: O, ’tis too true.

speaker: CLEON
text: But see what heaven can do by this our change:

speaker: CLEON
text: These mouths who but of late earth, sea, and air

speaker: CLEON
text: Were all too little to content and please,

speaker: CLEON
text: Although they gave their creatures in abundance,

speaker: CLEON
text: As houses are defiled for want of use,

speaker: CLEON
text: They are now starved for want of exercise.

speaker: CLEON
text: Those palates who not yet two savors younger

speaker: CLEON
text: Must have inventions to delight the taste,

speaker: CLEON
text: Would now be glad of bread and beg for it.

speaker: CLEON
text: Those mothers who, to nuzzle up their babes,

speaker: CLEON
text: Thought naught too curious, are ready now

speaker: CLEON
text: To eat those little darlings whom they loved.

speaker: CLEON
text: So sharp are hunger’s teeth that man and wife

speaker: CLEON
text: Draw lots who first shall die to lengthen life.

speaker: CLEON
text: Here stands a lord and there a lady weeping;

speaker: CLEON
text: Here many sink, yet those which see them fall

speaker: CLEON
text: Have scarce strength left to give them burial.

speaker: CLEON
text: Is not this true?

speaker: DIONYZA
text: Our cheeks and hollow eyes do witness it.

speaker: CLEON
text: O, let those cities that of Plenty’s cup

speaker: CLEON
text: And her prosperities so largely taste,

speaker: CLEON
text: With their superfluous riots, hear these tears.

speaker: CLEON
text: The misery of Tarsus may be theirs.

speaker: LORD
text: Where’s the Lord Governor?

speaker: CLEON
text: Here.

speaker: CLEON
text: Speak out thy sorrows, which thee bring’st in haste,

speaker: CLEON
text: For comfort is too far for us to expect.

speaker: LORD
text: We have descried upon our neighboring shore

speaker: LORD
text: A portly sail of ships make hitherward.

speaker: CLEON
text: I thought as much.

speaker: CLEON
text: One sorrow never comes but brings an heir

speaker: CLEON
text: That may succeed as his inheritor;

speaker: CLEON
text: And so in ours. Some neighboring nation,

speaker: CLEON
text: Taking advantage of our misery,

speaker: CLEON
text: Hath stuffed the hollow vessels with their power

speaker: CLEON
text: To beat us down, the which are down already,

speaker: CLEON
text: And make a conquest of unhappy men,

speaker: CLEON
text: Whereas no glory’s got to overcome.

speaker: LORD
text: That’s the least fear, for, by the semblance

speaker: LORD
text: Of their white flags displayed, they bring us peace

speaker: LORD
text: And come to us as favorers, not as foes.

speaker: CLEON
text: Thou speak’st like him’s untutored to repeat

speaker: CLEON
text: “Who makes the fairest show means most deceit.”

speaker: CLEON
text: But bring they what they will and what they can,

speaker: CLEON
text: What need we fear?

speaker: CLEON
text: The ground’s the lowest, and we are halfway there.

speaker: CLEON
text: Go tell their general we attend him here,

speaker: CLEON
text: To know for what he comes and whence he comes

speaker: CLEON
text: And what he craves.

speaker: LORD
text: I go, my lord.

speaker: CLEON
text: Welcome is peace, if he on peace consist;

speaker: CLEON
text: If wars, we are unable to resist.

speaker: PERICLES
text: Lord Governor, for so we hear you are,

speaker: PERICLES
text: Let not our ships and number of our men

speaker: PERICLES
text: Be like a beacon fired t’ amaze your eyes.

speaker: PERICLES
text: We have heard your miseries as far as Tyre

speaker: PERICLES
text: And seen the desolation of your streets;

speaker: PERICLES
text: Nor come we to add sorrow to your tears,

speaker: PERICLES
text: But to relieve them of their heavy load;

speaker: PERICLES
text: And these our ships, you happily may think

speaker: PERICLES
text: Are like the Trojan horse was stuffed within

speaker: PERICLES
text: With bloody veins expecting overthrow,

speaker: PERICLES
text: Are stored with corn to make your needy bread

speaker: PERICLES
text: And give them life whom hunger starved half dead.

speaker: ALL
text: The gods of Greece protect you, and we’ll pray for you.

speaker: PERICLES
text: Arise, I pray you, rise.

speaker: PERICLES
text: We do not look for reverence, but for love,

speaker: PERICLES
text: And harborage for ourself, our ships, and men.

speaker: CLEON
text: The which when any shall not gratify

speaker: CLEON
text: Or pay you with unthankfulness in thought,

speaker: CLEON
text: Be it our wives, our children, or ourselves,

speaker: CLEON
text: The curse of heaven and men succeed their evils!

speaker: CLEON
text: Till when—the which I hope shall ne’er be seen—

speaker: CLEON
text: Your Grace is welcome to our town and us.

speaker: PERICLES
text: Which welcome we’ll accept, feast here awhile,

speaker: PERICLES
text: Until our stars that frown lend us a smile.
